original_hypothesis.title: fastConfirmNewAssertion Repeated Calls Drain Rollup Contract Token Balance
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: fastConfirmNewAssertion can create assertions without requiring a staker deposit (no receiveTokens call). When the created assertion is not the first child, requiredStake is transferred from the rollup contract to loserStakeEscrow. A compromised anyTrustFastConfirmer could call this function repeatedly on the same predecessor, creating multiple non-first-child assertions, each draining requiredStake from the contract balance. This would exhaust the contract's tokens, preventing legitimate staker withdrawals. The code comments explicitly acknowledge this trust assumption.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The function creates assertions and transfers stake without corresponding deposits. The code comments note: 'We trust the anyTrustFastConfirmer to not call this function multiple times on the same prev'. This is a documented trust assumption with catastrophic failure mode.

proof_of_signal.pattern: createNewAssertion called without staker deposit; safeTransfer(loserStakeEscrow, requiredStake) on non-first-child with no deposit to cover it; comment acknowledges trust assumption

preconditions: anyTrustFastConfirmer address is compromised or malicious

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Assertion creation path that transfers tokens out without corresponding deposit in
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time the rollup is not paused and anyTrustFastConfirmer is compromised

operational_questions: What security controls protect the anyTrustFastConfirmer key/contract?
Can the admin replace anyTrustFastConfirmer immediately upon detecting compromise?
What is the maximum drainable amount?
validation_requirements: Verify that repeated fastConfirmNewAssertion calls drain balance
Quantify maximum drain rate per block
Test admin's ability to pause and replace fast confirmer

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call fastConfirmNewAssertion repeatedly with same prev, each creating non-first-child
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Each call transfers requiredStake to loserStakeEscrow from contract balance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Contract balance depleted, legitimate stakers unable to withdraw

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Full rollup state manipulation via fast confirmation bypass
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Token drain prevents all staker withdrawals; rollup insolvency
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires admin intervention to pause and replace fast confirmer; drained funds may be unrecoverable

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low once fast confirmer is compromised

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Multiple fastConfirmNewAssertion calls with same prevAssertion
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Rapid decline in contract token balance
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: fastConfirmNewAssertion called more than once per predecessor

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
